e1.goal: Create an agent name alias table (agent_authorities + agent_aliases) that enables order-insensitive, cross-script matching for bibliographic agent queries. This directly mirrors the existing publisher_authorities/publisher_variants pattern and leverages the 2,434 authority URIs and 1,937 Wikidata-linked enrichment records already in the database. By seeding aliases from authority_enrichment labels and Hebrew labels, queries like 'johann buxtorf', 'Moses Mendelssohn', 'Joseph Karo', and 'Maimonides' will resolve to all matching agent_norm values regardless of name order (surname-first vs given-first) or script (Latin vs Hebrew). The query adapter's AGENT_NORM handler (db_adapter.py lines 293-312) will be extended to JOIN through agent_aliases, converting single-string matching into authority-group matching that finds all records linked to the same canonical identity.

implementationSteps.description: Create the two new tables mirroring publisher_authorities/publisher_variants pattern. agent_authorities holds one row per canonical agent identity with fields: id, canonical_name, canonical_name_lower, agent_type (personal/corporate/meeting), dates_active, wikidata_id, viaf_id, nli_id, authority_uri (link to authority_enrichment), confidence, created_at, updated_at. agent_aliases holds one row per name variant with fields: id, authority_id (FK), alias_form, alias_form_lower, alias_type (primary/variant_spelling/cross_script/patronymic/acronym/word_reorder), script (latin/hebrew/arabic/other), language, is_primary, priority, notes, created_at. Add to m3_schema.sql as additive DDL with IF NOT EXISTS. Do NOT modify existing agents table.

implementationSteps.description: Create scripts/metadata/seed_agent_authorities.py that populates agent_authorities and agent_aliases from existing data. Strategy: (1) Group agents by authority_uri — each unique authority_uri becomes one authority record. (2) For each authority_uri, gather all distinct agent_norm values as primary aliases. (3) Look up authority_enrichment for the URI — add enrichment.label as 'variant_spelling' alias, add hebrew_label (from person_info JSON) as 'cross_script' alias. (4) For each Latin-script agent_norm in 'Last, First' format, generate a 'First Last' word-reorder alias. (5) Handle agents without authority_uri: create a standalone authority per distinct agent_norm with just one primary alias. (6) Deduplicate aliases (unique alias_form_lower constraint). The script should be idempotent (skip existing records) and log statistics: total authorities created, aliases per type, cross-script aliases found.
implementationSteps.testStrategy: Test with a small in-memory DB containing 5-10 agents with known authority_uri values and mock authority_enrichment rows. Verify correct number of authorities and aliases created. Test word-reorder: 'buxtorf, johann' -> alias 'johann buxtorf'. Test cross-script: Hebrew label from enrichment creates Hebrew alias.
implementationSteps.title: Implement alias seeding script

implementationSteps.description: Modify build_where_clause() in db_adapter.py to change the AGENT_NORM handler (lines 293-312). New logic: (1) Check if agent_aliases table exists (graceful fallback). (2) If alias table exists, use a subquery: find all authority_ids matching the search term via agent_aliases, then find all agent_norm values linked to those authorities (via agents.authority_uri = agent_authorities.authority_uri), then match records whose agent_norm is in that set. (3) Also OR-in the existing direct match (for agents without authority records). The SQL pattern becomes: EXISTS(SELECT 1 FROM agent_aliases al JOIN agent_authorities aa ON al.authority_id = aa.id JOIN agents a2 ON a2.authority_uri = aa.authority_uri WHERE a2.record_id = r.id AND al.alias_form_lower LIKE :param). This avoids modifying the main JOIN structure — the alias resolution is self-contained in an EXISTS subquery. The normalize_filter_value AGENT_NORM branch (lines 95-110) stays the same since it already casefolds and strips commas.

reportFailuresAddressed.currentBehavior: Query 'johann buxtorf' produces AGENT_NORM CONTAINS 'johann buxtorf'. DB has 'buxtorf, johann' (surname-first). LOWER(REPLACE(agent_norm, ',', '')) = 'buxtorf johann' which does not match 'johann buxtorf' because word order differs.
reportFailuresAddressed.expectedFix: Create agent_aliases entry linking 'buxtorf, johann' agent_norm to authority_id. Seed alias 'Johannes Buxtorf' from authority_enrichment.label and 'johann buxtorf' as word-reordered variant. Query 'johann buxtorf' resolves via alias table to authority_id, then JOINs to all agent_norm values under that authority.
reportFailuresAddressed.rootCause: The normalize_filter_value function (db_adapter.py:95-110) removes commas and casefolds, but the SQL LIKE/EQUALS comparison is still positional — 'johann buxtorf' != 'buxtorf johann'. No word-order normalization exists.

reportFailuresAddressed.currentBehavior: Query 'Moses Mendelssohn' produces AGENT_NORM CONTAINS 'moses mendelssohn'. DB has two forms: 'mendelssohn, moses' (Latin) and 'מנדלסון, משה' (Hebrew). Only the Latin form partially matches via comma-removal but word order still fails.
reportFailuresAddressed.expectedFix: Create authority record for Mendelssohn. Seed aliases: 'mendelssohn, moses', 'מנדלסון, משה', 'Moses Mendelssohn' (from enrichment label), 'משה מנדלסון' (from Hebrew label). Query resolves via any alias to authority_id, matching both Latin and Hebrew records.
reportFailuresAddressed.rootCause: Word-order mismatch (same as Q6) plus cross-script gap. The Hebrew form 'מנדלסון, משה' shares the same authority_uri but no alias linkage exists to connect a Latin query to the Hebrew agent_norm.

reportFailuresAddressed.currentBehavior: Query 'Maimonides' finds 7 records via 'maimonides, moses' agent_norm but misses 15+ records catalogued under 'משה בן מימון' (Hebrew patronymic form). Both share the same authority_uri but the query only matches the Latin form.
reportFailuresAddressed.expectedFix: Create authority record for Maimonides. Seed aliases from all sources: 'maimonides, moses', 'משה בן מימון', 'Moshe ben Maimon' (enrichment label), 'רמב"ם' (Hebrew acronym from enrichment). Query 'Maimonides' matches alias, resolves to authority_id, finds all 22+ records across both agent_norm values.
reportFailuresAddressed.rootCause: Two distinct agent_norm values ('maimonides, moses' and 'משה בן מימון') point to the same authority_uri (987007265654005171) but the current CONTAINS match on agent_norm cannot bridge across scripts. The enrichment table has label='Moshe ben Maimon' and hebrew_label='רמב"ם' but these are not used in query resolution.

reportFailuresAddressed.currentBehavior: Query 'Joseph Karo' produces AGENT_NORM CONTAINS 'joseph karo'. DB only has Hebrew form 'קארו, יוסף בן אפרים'. Zero records match because Latin search cannot find Hebrew-only agent_norm.
reportFailuresAddressed.expectedFix: Create authority record for Karo. Seed aliases: 'קארו, יוסף בן אפרים' (from agent_norm), 'Joseph ben Ephraim Karo' (from enrichment label), 'joseph karo' (simplified Latin form), 'יוסף קארו' (Hebrew label from enrichment). Query 'Joseph Karo' matches alias, resolves to authority_id, finds all Hebrew-catalogued records.
reportFailuresAddressed.rootCause: No Latin agent_norm exists for Karo — all records use the Hebrew form. The enrichment table has label='Joseph ben Ephraim Karo' and hebrew_label='יוסף קארו' linked to the authority_uri, but these are not used during query execution.

reportFailuresAddressed.currentBehavior: Query 'Aldine Press' may compile to PUBLISHER EQUALS or AGENT_NORM. The publisher_norm values include 'in aedibus aldi', 'apud aldum', 'aldine press, venice' etc. A simple EQUALS on 'aldine' or 'aldus' fails because the Latin forms are verbose and varied.
reportFailuresAddressed.expectedFix: Create agent authority for Aldus Manutius. Seed aliases: 'manuzio, aldo' (from agent_norm), 'Aldus Manutius' (common English form), 'Aldo Manuzio' (Italian form from enrichment). This handles the agent/printer dimension of Q3. The publisher dimension requires separate publisher_variants work (out of scope for E1 but complementary).
reportFailuresAddressed.rootCause: Q3 is primarily a publisher normalization issue (EQUALS vs CONTAINS, and variant publisher names). However, the agent side also has 'manuzio, aldo' in the agents table. An agent alias for 'Aldus Manutius' -> 'manuzio, aldo' would help queries that target the printer as a person rather than a publisher brand.

retrievalChanges.filterNormalization: Minimal changes to normalize_filter_value() (db_adapter.py:95-110). The existing AGENT_NORM normalization (casefold, remove brackets, remove commas, strip punctuation, collapse whitespace) is already correct for alias lookup since alias_form_lower is also lowercased. One addition: when constructing the alias lookup SQL, apply the same normalization to strip commas from the alias_form_lower comparison, ensuring 'joseph karo' matches an alias stored as 'karo, joseph'. Alternatively, store alias_form_lower already comma-stripped — but keeping commas in storage preserves the original form and is safer.
retrievalChanges.queryCompilation: No changes needed to the LLM compiler (scripts/query/llm_compiler.py). The system prompt already directs agent queries to use AGENT_NORM field with CONTAINS operation. The alias resolution happens entirely at the SQL execution layer, transparent to query planning. The LLM continues to output 'agent_norm' + 'CONTAINS' + 'joseph karo' and the adapter resolves it to matching records via aliases.
retrievalChanges.sqlGeneration: Major change to the AGENT_NORM branch of build_where_clause() (db_adapter.py:293-312). The current pattern generates: LOWER(REPLACE(a.agent_norm, ',', '')) LIKE LOWER(:param). The new pattern generates an OR of two conditions: (1) The existing direct match for backward compatibility: LOWER(REPLACE(a.agent_norm, ',', '')) LIKE :param, and (2) An EXISTS subquery for alias resolution: EXISTS(SELECT 1 FROM agent_aliases al JOIN agent_authorities aa ON al.authority_id = aa.id WHERE LOWER(REPLACE(al.alias_form_lower, ',', '')) LIKE :param AND aa.authority_uri = a.authority_uri). The second condition finds all agents sharing an authority_uri with any authority whose aliases match the search term. For CONTAINS operations, the LIKE with wildcards works. For EQUALS, exact match on alias_form_lower. The JOIN path is: agent_aliases -> agent_authorities (via authority_id) -> agents (via authority_uri match). This avoids adding new tables to the main FROM/JOIN clause and is self-contained.

risksAndEdgeCases.mitigation: The UNIQUE index on alias_form_lower means only one authority can own a given alias. For father/son cases like Buxtorf, they currently share the same agent_norm in the DB. Create a single authority with notes documenting the ambiguity. The alias resolves to all records for both father and son, which is acceptable recall behavior. Precision refinement (distinguishing father from son) is a separate enhancement.
risksAndEdgeCases.risk: Alias collision: two different people share the same name form (e.g., 'Johann Buxtorf I' and 'Johann Buxtorf II' both normalize to 'buxtorf, johann')

risksAndEdgeCases.mitigation: The UNIQUE index on alias_form_lower ensures O(1) lookup. The JOIN to agent_authorities via authority_id is indexed. The JOIN to agents via authority_uri is already indexed (idx_agents_authority_uri). Expected alias table size is ~8,000-10,000 rows — trivial for SQLite. Benchmark with EXPLAIN QUERY PLAN to verify index usage.
risksAndEdgeCases.risk: Performance: EXISTS subquery on agent_aliases may slow down queries if the alias table is large

risksAndEdgeCases.mitigation: The OR condition in the SQL preserves the existing direct match on agent_norm. Agents without authority_uri continue to work exactly as today. Standalone authority records are created for these agents (with authority_uri=NULL) so they can still have aliases (word-reorder variants). The alias subquery includes a fallback path: alias_form_lower direct match against the search term, with a secondary join to agents via a simple agent_norm comparison rather than authority_uri.
risksAndEdgeCases.risk: Agents without authority_uri (~700 distinct agent_norms) cannot be linked through the authority_uri join path

risksAndEdgeCases.mitigation: Hebrew characters are already stable under casefold() (no case distinction). The alias_form_lower is generated by Python's str.lower() which handles Hebrew correctly. Test with actual Hebrew strings from the database (e.g., 'משה בן מימון', 'קארו, יוסף בן אפרים'). Add specific unit tests for Hebrew alias storage and retrieval.
risksAndEdgeCases.risk: Hebrew text normalization: casefold() on Hebrew text may not behave as expected for all Unicode ranges

risksAndEdgeCases.mitigation: Use INSERT OR IGNORE for aliases (UNIQUE index on alias_form_lower catches duplicates). For authorities, check existence by authority_uri before creating. The seed script should be explicitly idempotent with logging of skipped records vs newly created records.
risksAndEdgeCases.risk: Seeding idempotency: running the seed script multiple times should not create duplicate records

risksAndEdgeCases.mitigation: All DDL uses CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS and CREATE INDEX IF NOT EXISTS. No existing tables are modified. The init_schema() method can be run safely on an existing database. The fallback mechanism (_agent_alias_tables_exist) means queries work correctly even before tables are created or populated.
risksAndEdgeCases.risk: Schema migration: existing production database needs the new tables added without disrupting existing data

risksAndEdgeCases.mitigation: Only apply word-reorder to names matching the simple 'Last, First' pattern (exactly one comma, Latin script). Skip names with multiple commas, Hebrew/Arabic script, or complex particles. Keep the original agent_norm as a primary alias regardless. Test with the actual top-20 most frequent agent_norm values to verify correctness.
risksAndEdgeCases.risk: Word-reorder alias generation may produce incorrect splits for complex names like 'de la Cruz, Juan' or Hebrew patronymics like 'משה בן מימון'

risksAndEdgeCases.mitigation: Short/ambiguous names should not be added as aliases. The seeding script should have a minimum length threshold (>3 characters) and skip single-word common names. The word-reorder and cross-script aliases are longer and more specific, reducing collision risk. If a collision is detected during seeding, log a warning and skip the duplicate alias (the first authority to claim it wins).
risksAndEdgeCases.risk: The UNIQUE constraint on alias_form_lower prevents the same name form from being an alias for two different authorities, but edge cases exist (e.g., 'Moses' could refer to multiple people)

schemaChanges.description: One row per canonical agent identity. Links to authority_enrichment via authority_uri. Stores external IDs directly for fast lookup. agent_type mirrors the agents table CHECK constraint. The authority_uri column connects this table to the agents table (agents.authority_uri = agent_authorities.authority_uri) enabling the critical join path for query resolution.
schemaChanges.seedDataSource: Derived from agents table grouped by authority_uri, enriched with authority_enrichment data (wikidata_id, viaf_id, label). Agents without authority_uri get standalone authority records.
schemaChanges.sql:
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS agent_authorities (
    id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT,
    canonical_name TEXT NOT NULL,
    canonical_name_lower TEXT NOT NULL,
    agent_type TEXT NOT NULL CHECK(agent_type IN ('personal', 'corporate', 'meeting')),
    dates_active TEXT,
    date_start INTEGER,
    date_end INTEGER,
    notes TEXT,
    sources TEXT,
    confidence REAL NOT NULL DEFAULT 0.5,
    authority_uri TEXT,
    wikidata_id TEXT,
    viaf_id TEXT,
    nli_id TEXT,
    created_at TEXT NOT NULL,
    updated_at TEXT NOT NULL
);

schemaChanges.description: Indexes for efficient lookup by canonical name (unique, case-insensitive), agent type filtering, authority URI JOIN, and Wikidata ID lookup.

One row per name variant, FK to agent_authorities. alias_type categorizes the relationship: primary (exact agent_norm value), variant_spelling (enrichment label), cross_script (Hebrew/Arabic form), patronymic (Hebrew patronymic), acronym (e.g. Rambam), word_reorder (first-last from last-first), historical (historical name form). The alias_form_lower column is the key lookup field used in query resolution.
schemaChanges.seedDataSource: Primary aliases from agents.agent_norm values. Variant aliases from authority_enrichment.label. Cross-script aliases from authority_enrichment.person_info->hebrew_label. Word-reorder aliases generated by splitting 'Last, First' -> 'First Last'.
schemaChanges.sql:
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS agent_aliases (
    id INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT,
    authority_id INTEGER NOT NULL REFERENCES agent_authorities(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
    alias_form TEXT NOT NULL,
    alias_form_lower TEXT NOT NULL,
    alias_type TEXT NOT NULL CHECK(alias_type IN (
        'primary', 'variant_spelling', 'cross_script',
        'patronymic', 'acronym', 'word_reorder', 'historical'
    )),
    script TEXT DEFAULT 'latin',
    language TEXT,
    is_primary INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
    priority INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 0,
    notes TEXT,
    created_at TEXT NOT NULL
);

e2.goal: Add analytical query routing so that questions requesting aggregation, distribution analysis, or curation are detected at Phase 1 (query definition) and routed directly to the existing aggregation engine rather than treated as standard retrieval queries. This eliminates the three 1/25-scoring failures (Q14 chronological shape, Q15 printing centers, Q20 curated exhibit) by detecting analytical intent before the intent agent is invoked, running the appropriate aggregation/summary pipeline over the full collection or filtered subset, and formatting results as structured breakdowns (histograms, ranked lists) instead of dumping thousands of raw records. The narrative agent's _MAX_RESULT_SET=100 ceiling is bypassed entirely because analytical queries never produce a CandidateSet for narrative -- they produce aggregation tables.

implementationSteps.description: Create scripts/chat/analytical_router.py with the core data model. AnalyticalIntent is an enum with values: TEMPORAL_DISTRIBUTION, GEOGRAPHIC_DISTRIBUTION, PUBLISHER_DISTRIBUTION, LANGUAGE_DISTRIBUTION, SUBJECT_DISTRIBUTION, AGENT_DISTRIBUTION, GENERAL_DISTRIBUTION, CURATION, NOT_ANALYTICAL. AnalyticalQueryResult is a Pydantic model containing: is_analytical (bool), intent (AnalyticalIntent), aggregation_field (Optional[str] mapped from intent, e.g. TEMPORAL_DISTRIBUTION -> 'date_decade'), implied_filters (List[Filter] extracted from the query text like 'Hebrew' -> language=heb), aggregation_limit (int, default 20 for distributions, 15 for curation), confidence (float). This step has zero dependencies and establishes the contract for all downstream consumers.
implementationSteps.testStrategy: Unit test: instantiate AnalyticalQueryResult with various intents, verify aggregation_field mapping, verify serialization. Test that NOT_ANALYTICAL always has is_analytical=False.
implementationSteps.title: Define AnalyticalIntent enum and AnalyticalQueryResult model

implementationSteps.description: Add the primary detection function to scripts/chat/analytical_router.py. This function uses a two-layer approach: (1) Signal detection: scan for analytical signal words organized by category -- temporal signals ('chronological', 'distribution over time', 'by decade', 'by century', 'temporal', 'when were.*published', 'date distribution'), geographic signals ('printing centers', 'where were.*printed', 'geographic distribution', 'by city', 'by place', 'by country', 'place distribution'), publisher signals ('top publishers', 'publisher distribution', 'who published', 'most prolific'), curation signals ('curated', 'exhibit', 'exhibition', 'display', 'selection for', 'best examples', 'highlights', 'showcase'). (2) Anti-signals: if the query also matches retrieval patterns like 'show me books about X', 'find', 'list all', 'search for' without distribution keywords, return NOT_ANALYTICAL. The function returns AnalyticalQueryResult. Implied filters are extracted from the query text using a lightweight pattern matcher (not LLM): language names map to language codes, known place names map to imprint_place filters, date ranges map to year RANGE filters. This reuses the same keyword lists that is_overview_query() uses for search_criteria detection (lines 706-727 of aggregation.py) but interprets them as filter extraction rather than exclusion.
implementationSteps.testStrategy: Comprehensive unit tests covering: (a) Q14-style: 'chronological distribution of the collection' -> TEMPORAL_DISTRIBUTION, field='date_decade', no filters; (b) Q15-style: 'major Hebrew printing centers represented' -> GEOGRAPHIC_DISTRIBUTION, field='place', filters=[language=heb]; (c) Q20-style: 'curated selection for Hebrew printing exhibit' -> CURATION, filters=[subject CONTAINS 'hebrew printing']; (d) False negatives: 'books printed in Paris' -> NOT_ANALYTICAL; (e) Edge case: 'show me the date distribution of Latin books' -> TEMPORAL_DISTRIBUTION with language=lat filter; (f) 'compare Venice vs Amsterdam' -> NOT_ANALYTICAL (this is comparison, not aggregation).
implementationSteps.title: Implement detect_analytical_query() with keyword/pattern matching

implementationSteps.description: The existing execute_aggregation() requires a list of record_ids. For full-collection analytical queries (like Q14 'chronological shape of the collection'), we need to either pass ALL record IDs or use an optimized query that doesn't use the IN clause. Add execute_aggregation_full_collection(db_path, field, limit) that runs the aggregation SQL without the WHERE record_id IN (...) clause, using a separate set of FULL_COLLECTION_AGGREGATION_QUERIES templates. Also add get_all_record_ids(db_path) -> List[str] as a utility for cases where we need the full list (e.g., when implied filters exist and we need to first filter then aggregate). The full-collection variant avoids the performance issue of passing 2,796+ parameters in an IN clause.
implementationSteps.testStrategy: Unit test with in-memory SQLite: create test table, insert 100 rows, verify execute_aggregation_full_collection returns correct counts grouped by field. Test that get_all_record_ids returns all MMS IDs. Test that full-collection results match execute_aggregation(all_ids, field) results.
implementationSteps.title: Add execute_aggregation_full_collection() to aggregation.py

implementationSteps.description: Add a new async function handle_analytical_query(analytical_result, chat_request, session, store, bib_db) to app/api/main.py. This function: (1) If analytical_result has implied_filters, uses the intent agent to compile them into a QueryPlan, executes via QueryService to get the filtered CandidateSet, then runs execute_aggregation() on those record_ids. (2) If no implied_filters (full collection), calls execute_aggregation_full_collection(). (3) For CURATION intent, delegates to the curation engine (Step 7). (4) Formats the aggregation response using format_aggregation_response(). (5) Creates a ChatResponse with phase=CORPUS_EXPLORATION (so follow-up questions can use the subgroup), visualization_hint, and structured data. (6) Stores an ActiveSubgroup so the user can continue exploring. The key difference from Phase 2 aggregation is that this runs inline during Phase 1, before any retrieval results are shown. The response message leads with the analytical answer, not with 'Found N records'.
implementationSteps.testStrategy: Integration test with mocked QueryService: verify that an analytical query routes to handle_analytical_query, produces an aggregation response with visualization_hint, transitions session to CORPUS_EXPLORATION phase. Test the implied-filter path (Q15: Hebrew + place aggregation) and the full-collection path (Q14: date_decade aggregation).
implementationSteps.title: Implement handle_analytical_query() in main.py

implementationSteps.description: Modify handle_query_definition_phase() in main.py to call detect_analytical_query() after the is_overview_query() check but BEFORE interpret_query(). The new routing order at the top of handle_query_definition_phase() becomes: (1) is_overview_query() -> return collection overview (existing, line 450), (2) detect_analytical_query() -> return handle_analytical_query() (NEW), (3) interpret_query() -> standard retrieval flow (existing, line 487). This placement ensures analytical queries are caught early without an LLM call, saving latency and API costs. The detect_analytical_query() function is purely deterministic (keyword matching), just like is_overview_query(). Import detect_analytical_query and handle_analytical_query at the top of main.py.
implementationSteps.testStrategy: Unit test: mock detect_analytical_query to return is_analytical=True, verify handle_query_definition_phase calls handle_analytical_query. Mock detect_analytical_query to return is_analytical=False, verify it falls through to interpret_query. implementationSteps.title: Wire detect_analytical_query() into handle_query_definition_phase()

implementationSteps.description: The websocket_chat() handler at main.py:1130 has its own routing logic that currently calls compile_query() directly (line 1193) rather than going through handle_query_definition_phase(). Add analytical query detection to the WebSocket handler: after 'Compiling query...' progress message, call detect_analytical_query(). If analytical, send progress 'Analyzing collection...' then run the aggregation, stream the result as a complete response (no batching needed for aggregation). This ensures both HTTP and WebSocket clients get analytical routing.

implementationSteps.description: Create scripts/chat/curation_engine.py with a deterministic scoring heuristic for selecting items for exhibits/showcases. CurationScorer takes a list of record MMS IDs and scores each using: (a) temporal_score: earlier dates score higher (incunabula and early printed books are rarer/more exhibit-worthy), normalized to 0-1; (b) enrichment_score: records with agent enrichment data (Wikidata bios, images) score higher because they have more exhibit narrative potential; (c) diversity_bonus: records from underrepresented places/publishers in the set get a bonus to ensure variety; (d) subject_richness: records with more subject headings score higher. All scores are weighted: temporal=0.3, enrichment=0.3, diversity=0.2, subject_richness=0.2. select_curated_items(db_path, record_ids, n=15) returns the top-N scored items with per-item rationale strings (e.g., 'Early Venice printing (1492), has enrichment data for Daniel Bomberg'). format_curation_response() produces a markdown response with numbered items and rationale.
implementationSteps.testStrategy: Unit test with in-memory DB: create 50 test records with varying dates, enrichment presence, places. Verify that select_curated_items(n=10) returns 10 items, that earlier dates rank higher, that diversity is maintained (not all from same place), that rationale strings are non-empty. Test edge case: fewer records than n requested.
implementationSteps.title: Implement curation engine for Q20-style queries

implementationSteps.description: Modify narrative_agent.py to handle analytical contexts. Currently generate_agent_narrative() returns None when len(candidates) > _MAX_RESULT_SET (line 220). Add an optional parameter analytical_mode=False. When analytical_mode=True, instead of generating per-agent bios, generate a brief statistical summary of agents in the result set: 'This set includes works by N distinct agents, including M printers, P authors...' using a lightweight SQL count query. This is NOT about raising _MAX_RESULT_SET -- it's about providing a different kind of narrative for analytical responses. The analytical narrative is appended to the aggregation response by handle_analytical_query() when the filtered subset has enrichment data. For Q14 (full collection, 2796 records), this might say 'Spanning 2,796 works by over 800 distinct agents.' For Q15 (806 Hebrew records), it might note the top 3 agents.
implementationSteps.testStrategy: Unit test: call generate_analytical_narrative with a mock CandidateSet of 500 records, verify it returns a summary string (not None). Verify the original generate_agent_narrative still returns None for sets >100 when analytical_mode=False (backward compatibility).
implementationSteps.title: Add analytical summary path to narrative_agent.py

implementationSteps.description: In Phase 2, after an analytical response, the user might ask follow-up analytical questions like 'now show me the publisher breakdown' or 'what about by century instead'. The exploration agent already handles AGGREGATION intent well in Phase 2, so this step is lightweight: (1) Add 'CURATION' to ExplorationIntent enum in models.py and to the intent types in EXPLORATION_AGENT_SYSTEM_PROMPT. (2) Add CURATION handler in handle_corpus_exploration_phase() that calls select_curated_items() on the active subgroup's record_ids. (3) Update the EXPLORATION_AGENT_SYSTEM_PROMPT to include examples of curation requests. This ensures that even if the initial query was retrieval but the follow-up is analytical, it routes correctly.
implementationSteps.testStrategy: Unit test: verify ExplorationIntent.CURATION exists and is serializable. Integration test: start with a retrieval query, then send 'curate the best items for an exhibit' as a follow-up, verify it routes to CURATION handler.

(2) Verify the response contains aggregation data (not raw candidates). (3) Verify visualization_hint is set. (4) Verify the message contains structured breakdown text (decade counts for Q14, place counts for Q15, curated items for Q20). (5) Verify session transitions to CORPUS_EXPLORATION. reportFailuresAddressed.currentBehavior: Returns all 2,796 records with no temporal analysis. The intent agent classifies 'chronological distribution of the collection' as a retrieval query, executes it with no filters, and the narrative agent silently skips the result set because it exceeds _MAX_RESULT_SET=100. Score: 1/25.
reportFailuresAddressed.expectedFix: A new detect_analytical_query() function intercepts the query before interpret_query() is called. It detects 'chronological distribution' as an analytical query with implied aggregation_field='date_decade'. The system runs execute_aggregation() with field='date_decade' over ALL record MMS IDs (full collection query), then format_aggregation_response() produces a decade-by-decade breakdown. The response includes visualization_hint='bar_chart' and structured data. No CandidateSet is returned, so _MAX_RESULT_SET is irrelevant.
reportFailuresAddressed.rootCause: is_overview_query() at aggregation.py:688 does not match analytical keywords like 'chronological', 'distribution', 'shape'. The intent agent (intent_agent.py) has no ANALYTICAL intent type -- it only produces retrieval-oriented QueryPlans with filters. handle_query_definition_phase() at main.py:424 checks is_overview_query() first (line 450) but that function returns False for analytical questions. The query then falls through to interpret_query() which generates a plan with no filters, still proceeds to execution (because proceed_to_execution is True even with empty filters if confidence is high enough), and returns all records.

reportFailuresAddressed.currentBehavior: Returns 806 Hebrew records with no geographic aggregation. The intent agent correctly extracts language=heb filter but treats it as retrieval. The narrative agent skips the 806-record set. Score: 1/25.
reportFailuresAddressed.expectedFix: detect_analytical_query() identifies 'printing centers' as analytical with aggregation_field='place'. It also detects the implicit filter (language=heb from 'Hebrew'). The system first executes the retrieval query to get the 806 Hebrew records, then immediately runs execute_aggregation(field='place') on that subset, and returns the place-by-count breakdown. The response message says 'Here are the major printing centers for Hebrew books in the collection' with a ranked list.
reportFailuresAddressed.rootCause: Same routing gap as Q14. 'Major Hebrew printing centers represented' is an analytical question that needs: (1) filter to Hebrew books, (2) aggregate by place, (3) return ranked place-by-count. The intent agent produces a valid language filter but has no mechanism to also specify that the user wants aggregation over the results. handle_query_definition_phase() executes the plan and returns 806 raw candidates.

reportFailuresAddressed.currentBehavior: Returns 120 records for 'curated selection for Hebrew printing exhibit' with no selection or curation capability. The narrative agent skips the set (>100 records). Score: 1/25.
reportFailuresAddressed.expectedFix: detect_analytical_query() identifies 'curated selection' / 'exhibit' as a CURATION intent. The system first retrieves matching records (subject CONTAINS 'hebrew printing'), then applies a multi-criteria scoring heuristic: earliest date (historical significance), has_enrichment (biographical context available), diversity of places/publishers (exhibit variety), and presence of subjects (topical richness). The top N items are returned with explanatory notes. This is a deterministic heuristic, not an LLM selection -- keeping with the project's evidence-first philosophy.
reportFailuresAddressed.rootCause: The system has no recommendation/curation intent type in Phase 1. The query is treated as standard retrieval matching 'Hebrew printing' subject terms. Even in Phase 2, the RECOMMENDATION intent handler returns a static 'not yet available' message (main.py:1025-1038). There is no scoring/ranking mechanism to select the 10-15 most significant items.

retrievalChanges.filterNormalization: The implied_filters extraction in detect_analytical_query() reuses existing normalization logic: language names are mapped to ISO 639-2 codes (same mapping as INTENT_AGENT_SYSTEM_PROMPT lines 187-188), place names are lowercased (consistent with place_norm in imprints table), and date ranges use the same century conversion (15th = 1401-1500). When implied_filters need to be executed as a retrieval query, they are converted to a QueryPlan and run through the standard QueryService.execute_plan() pipeline, ensuring all existing normalization and SQL generation is reused.
retrievalChanges.queryCompilation: No changes to the LLM-based query compiler (scripts/query/llm_compiler.py) or intent agent (scripts/chat/intent_agent.py). Analytical queries are intercepted BEFORE the intent agent is called, so the IntentInterpretationLLM schema does not need an ANALYTICAL intent type. This keeps the LLM prompt focused on retrieval queries where it excels, and avoids the risk of the LLM misclassifying borderline queries. The deterministic keyword matcher handles analytical detection with higher reliability than prompt engineering.
retrievalChanges.sqlGeneration: execute_aggregation_full_collection() introduces new SQL templates that omit the WHERE record_id IN (...) clause. These are variants of the existing AGGREGATION_QUERIES dict (aggregation.py lines 33-141) with the IN clause removed. The GROUP BY, ORDER BY, and LIMIT clauses remain identical. For filtered analytical queries (Q15: Hebrew printing centers), the existing execute_aggregation() is used unchanged -- the filter step produces a record_ids list that feeds into the existing parameterized queries.

risksAndEdgeCases.mitigation: Use multi-word signal phrases rather than single words. 'chronological' alone is not a signal -- 'chronological distribution' or 'chronological shape' is. Add retrieval anti-signals: if the query contains 'about', 'on the topic of', 'books about' before the analytical keyword, classify as retrieval. Include a confidence threshold: if the analytical signal is weak (single keyword match), fall through to intent agent. Add a comprehensive test suite with 20+ borderline cases.
risksAndEdgeCases.risk: False positive analytical detection: a retrieval query like 'books about the chronological history of printing' might match 'chronological' and 'printing' signals and be misclassified as analytical

risksAndEdgeCases.mitigation: Start with conservative keyword lists covering the known failing queries (Q14, Q15, Q20) and common academic analytical terms. Monitor missed detections via logging (log when intent agent produces a plan with empty/broad filters AND large result sets -- these are likely missed analytical queries). Expand keyword lists iteratively based on real usage patterns. Consider adding an LLM-based fallback classifier in a future iteration.
risksAndEdgeCases.risk: False negative: novel analytical phrasing not covered by keyword lists (e.g., 'temporal profile of the collection', 'geographic footprint')

risksAndEdgeCases.mitigation: The aggregation queries use GROUP BY on indexed columns (place_norm, publisher_norm, date_start). SQLite handles 3K-record aggregations in <100ms. Add execution time logging. For future scaling, consider materialized views or pre-computed aggregation tables. The current collection size (2,796) is well within safe limits.
risksAndEdgeCases.risk: Full-collection aggregation performance: execute_aggregation_full_collection() on 2,796 records should be fast, but could be slow on larger collections

risksAndEdgeCases.mitigation: The heuristic is explicitly documented as deterministic and transparent. Each selected item includes a rationale string explaining why it was chosen. The scoring weights (temporal=0.3, enrichment=0.3, diversity=0.2, subject=0.2) are configurable. The response includes a caveat: 'This is an algorithmic selection based on date, enrichment data, and diversity. A specialist may want to adjust the selection.' Future enhancement: allow user-specified criteria ('prioritize visual items', 'focus on 16th century').
risksAndEdgeCases.risk: Curation scoring heuristic may not align with scholarly judgment about what is 'exhibit-worthy'

risksAndEdgeCases.mitigation: Use the same conservative extraction as is_overview_query's search_criteria list (aggregation.py lines 706-727). If a filter term is ambiguous, don't extract it -- let the aggregation run over the full collection or broader set. Log unrecognized terms for future mapping expansion. The implied filter extraction is best-effort; missing a filter produces a broader (but still useful) aggregation rather than an error.
risksAndEdgeCases.risk: Implied filter extraction from analytical queries may miss or misparse filters (e.g., 'Hebrew' detected as language but 'Sephardic' is not a recognized filter term)

risksAndEdgeCases.mitigation: Extract the analytical detection + handling into a shared function (handle_analytical_query) called by both code paths. The WebSocket handler adds progress messages around the shared function call. Test both paths with the same query inputs to verify identical analytical responses.
risksAndEdgeCases.risk: WebSocket and HTTP routing divergence: analytical detection must be added to both handlers, risking inconsistency

risksAndEdgeCases.mitigation: For full-collection analytical queries, set active_subgroup.record_ids to all record IDs (via get_all_record_ids()). The Phase 2 aggregation engine already handles large record_id lists via parameterized queries. Alternatively, set a flag on the ActiveSubgroup indicating it represents the full collection, and optimize Phase 2 queries accordingly. The 2,796-record limit is well within SQLite parameterized query limits.
risksAndEdgeCases.risk: Session phase transition after analytical response: user is in CORPUS_EXPLORATION but the active_subgroup may reference the full collection (for Q14-style queries), leading to Phase 2 aggregations running over 2,796 records

schemaChanges.description: Add CURATION value to ExplorationIntent enum in scripts/chat/models.py (line 39-51). No database schema changes required. No API response schema changes -- ChatResponse already has metadata dict for visualization_hint and data fields.

e3.goal: Build an Entity Cross-Reference and Set Comparison Engine that discovers and surfaces relationships between agents (authors, printers, translators) across query result sets, and enriches comparison operations with multi-faceted side-by-side analysis. This addresses 8 queries (Q1, Q2, Q4, Q5, Q9, Q10, Q13, Q17) that currently lack cross-referencing and comparison capability, collectively scoring 108/200 (54%) where 160/200 (80%) is achievable.

implementationSteps.description: Add Connection, AgentNode, ComparisonResult, and ComparisonFacets Pydantic models. Add CROSS_REFERENCE to ExplorationIntent enum.
details: Connection model: agent_a (str), agent_b (str), relationship_type (enum: teacher_of, student_of, co_publication, same_place_period, shared_occupation, same_teacher), evidence (str with source citation), confidence (float 0-1), wikidata_ids (optional tuple for both agents)

AgentNode model: label (str), agent_norm (str), authority_uri (optional str), wikidata_id (optional str), birth_year (optional int), death_year (optional int), birth_place (optional str), occupations (List[str]), teachers (List[str]), students (List[str]), notable_works (List[str]), record_count (int)

ComparisonFacets model: counts (Dict[str, int]), date_ranges (Dict[str, tuple[int,int]]), language_distribution (Dict[str, Dict[str, int]]), top_agents (Dict[str, List[tuple[str,int]]]), subject_overlap (List[str]), shared_agents (List[str])
ComparisonResult model: field (str), values (List[str]), facets (ComparisonFacets), total_in_subgroup (int)
Add CROSS_REFERENCE = 'cross_reference' to ExplorationIntent enum at line 48
implementationSteps.title: Define data models for connections and comparisons

implementationSteps.description: New module implementing the relationship graph and connection discovery. Pure functions operating on SQLite data, no LLM calls.
details: build_agent_graph(db_path: Path) -> dict[str, AgentNode]: Loads all authority_enrichment records with non-null person_info, constructs in-memory lookup by label. Currently 2,665 enriched records, 277 with non-empty teachers, 250 with non-empty students.

find_connections(record_ids: list[str], db_path: Path, max_connections: int = 20) -> list[Connection]: Main entry point. (1) Fetches agent enrichment for record_ids using get_agent_enrichment_for_records() from narrative_agent.py. (2) For each pair of agents in result set, checks: teacher/student links via person_info.teachers and person_info.students lists, co-publication via shared record_ids, same birth_place or nationality, shared occupation. (3) Also checks if any agent's teachers/students match OTHER agents in the full authority_enrichment table (not just result set) -- this discovers 'network neighbors' not in the current result set.

find_network_neighbors(agent_labels: list[str], db_path: Path, max_hops: int = 1) -> list[Connection]: Given agent labels from result set, find agents 1 hop away in the teacher/student graph who are also in authority_enrichment. Returns Connection objects with relationship_type='network_neighbor'. The 85 cross-referenced teacher-student pairs found in DB confirm this is viable.

_match_teacher_student(agent_a: dict, agent_b: dict) -> Optional[Connection]: Check if agent_a.students contains agent_b.label or vice versa. Uses fuzzy matching (label substring) since Wikidata student names may not exactly match authority_enrichment labels.

_match_co_publication(agents_with_records: list[dict]) -> list[Connection]: Find agent pairs sharing >1 record_id. DB shows rich co-publication data (e.g., 38 shared records for Faitlovitch/Wurmbrand pair). Filter to pairs with different roles (author+printer, author+translator) for more meaningful connections.

_match_same_place_period(agent_a: dict, agent_b: dict, tolerance_years: int = 30) -> Optional[Connection]: Match agents active in same city during overlapping lifetimes. Uses birth_place, death_place from person_info and birth_year/death_year for temporal overlap.
Graph caching: Build graph once per API startup (lazy singleton pattern). Graph is ~2,665 nodes, fits easily in memory. Invalidation: rebuild on DB change or manual trigger.

risksAndEdgeCases.description: person_info.teachers/students contain display labels (e.g., 'Nahmanides') while authority_enrichment.label may have fuller forms (e.g., 'Naḥmanides, 1194-1270'). Substring matching may produce false positives (e.g., 'Isaac' matching multiple Isaacs).
risksAndEdgeCases.mitigation: Use a two-pass matching strategy: (1) exact label match first, (2) if no match, try normalized substring match with additional constraints (same century, same geographic region). Track match quality in Connection.confidence (exact=0.95, fuzzy=0.75). Add test cases for known ambiguous names.
risksAndEdgeCases.risk: Name matching ambiguity in teacher/student cross-referencing

risksAndEdgeCases.description: For a result set with N agents, pairwise comparison is O(N^2). A result set with 50 agents produces 1,225 pairs to check. The _MAX_RESULT_SET guard of 100 records in narrative_agent helps but doesn't limit agent count.
risksAndEdgeCases.mitigation: Set max_agents_for_cross_ref = 30 (435 pairs max). If more agents, take top 30 by record_count. Co-publication query uses a single SQL GROUP BY instead of N^2 individual queries. Teacher/student check is O(N * avg_teachers) using pre-built graph lookup.
risksAndEdgeCases.risk: Performance of pairwise agent comparison in large result sets

risksAndEdgeCases.description: In-memory agent graph is built at startup. If authority_enrichment is updated (new Wikidata enrichment run), the graph becomes stale.
risksAndEdgeCases.mitigation: Lazy singleton with optional rebuild trigger. Since enrichment runs are rare (manual process), staleness is acceptable. Add /admin/rebuild-graph endpoint for manual refresh. Log graph build timestamp in health check.
risksAndEdgeCases.risk: Graph staleness after DB updates

risksAndEdgeCases.description: Only 277/5,667 authority records have non-empty teachers lists, 250 have students. Many result sets will produce zero connections.
risksAndEdgeCases.mitigation: Fall back gracefully: if no teacher/student connections found, try co-publication and same_place_period which use the agents table directly (2,434 distinct authority URIs). If still no connections, return empty list and skip Connections section in narrative. Never show an empty 'No connections found' message.
risksAndEdgeCases.risk: Sparse enrichment data producing empty connections

risksAndEdgeCases.description: Enhanced comparison runs 5 SQL queries per compared value plus 2 cross-value queries. For a subgroup of 500 records comparing 3 values, that's 17 queries.
risksAndEdgeCases.mitigation: All queries filter by mms_id IN (...) with parameterized placeholders, hitting indexed columns. SQLite handles IN clauses efficiently for < 1000 values. Add timing logs. If latency > 2s, add a fast-path that returns just counts (backward-compatible).
risksAndEdgeCases.risk: Enhanced comparison SQL performance with large subgroups

risksAndEdgeCases.description: Unlikely in real data but possible: A teaches B who teaches A. find_network_neighbors() with hops > 1 could loop.
risksAndEdgeCases.mitigation: Track visited nodes in a set during graph traversal. Max hops = 1 by default eliminates cycles. Test with synthetic circular data.
risksAndEdgeCases.risk: Circular references in teacher/student graph

risksAndEdgeCases.description: User says 'Compare Buxtorf and Wasmuth' -- is this a comparison (side-by-side stats) or cross-reference (relationship discovery)? The LLM may choose wrong intent.
risksAndEdgeCases.mitigation: When COMPARISON is requested with comparison_field='agent', automatically include cross-reference data in the response alongside comparison facets. This way both intents produce useful results regardless of classification. System prompt examples clearly distinguish the two: COMPARISON = 'Compare Venice vs Paris', CROSS_REFERENCE = 'Show connections between these authors'.
risksAndEdgeCases.risk: LLM misclassification between COMPARISON and CROSS_REFERENCE intents

e4.goal: Add scholarly narrative depth to 7 queries (Q1, Q2, Q4, Q5, Q11, Q16, Q18) that currently score 9-15/25 on the historian evaluation due to THIN_NARRATIVE and missing pedagogical framing. Introduce a thematic context layer with pre-authored, cited historical paragraphs; a significance scoring function for highlighting notable items; and pedagogical framing for teaching-oriented queries. All thematic content is deterministic, human-authored prose with citations -- never LLM-generated fabrication.

implementationSteps.description: Create Pydantic models: ThematicBlock (theme_id, title, context_paragraph, citations, teaching_note, matched_by), SignificanceResult (record_id, score, factors), NotableItem (record_id, score, reason, highlight_text). ThematicBlock.context_paragraph is the pre-authored prose. ThematicBlock.citations is a list of scholarly references (author, title, year). ThematicBlock.matched_by records which filter dimensions triggered the match.
implementationSteps.title: Define data models for thematic context

implementationSteps.description: Write 8 pre-authored thematic context entries as a Python dict (THEMATIC_REGISTRY). Each entry is a ThematicEntry dataclass with: theme_id (str), match_rules (list of MatchRule with field/value/date_range conditions), context_paragraph (str, 3-5 sentences of scholarly prose), citations (list of Reference), teaching_note (Optional[str]). The 8 themes are: (1) venetian_hebrew_printing -- Bomberg, Bragadin, Council of Trent, censorship; (2) amsterdam_dutch_jerusalem -- Menasseh ben Israel, Sephardic refugees, freedom of press; (3) christian_hebraism -- Buxtorf, Reuchlin, Pfefferkorn controversy; (4) haskalah -- Mendelssohn, Berlin Haskalah, secularization; (5) incunabula_spread -- Gutenberg impact, first Hebrew printed books, Soncino; (6) talmud_printing -- Bomberg 1520-23, 1553 burning, Slavuta/Vilna; (7) napoleonic_emancipation -- Sanhedrin, ghetto dissolution, secular publishing; (8) ottoman_hebrew_printing -- Constantinople, Salonika, Sephardic diaspora. Each paragraph must clearly cite reference works (e.g., Heller 2004, Offenberg 1990, Fuks 1987) and be labeled as historical context.
implementationSteps.designNotes: These paragraphs are the scholarly heart of E4. They must be authored by hand with domain expertise, not generated by LLM. Each paragraph should be factually verifiable against the cited references. The match_rules use the same FilterField enum from scripts/schemas/query_plan.py to ensure type safety.
implementationSteps.title: Author thematic context entries

implementationSteps.description: Function get_thematic_context(filters: List[Filter], candidates: List[Candidate], db_path: Path) -> Optional[ThematicBlock]. Algorithm: (1) Extract filter dimensions from the query plan filters (place values, date ranges, subject keywords, publisher values, language). (2) For each ThematicEntry in THEMATIC_REGISTRY, evaluate match_rules against extracted dimensions. A match_rule specifies required field matches (e.g., place_norm IN ['venice'], date_range OVERLAPS 1500-1600, subject CONTAINS 'talmud'). (3) Score each theme by number of matching dimensions. (4) Return the highest-scoring theme if score >= threshold (at least 1 strong match). (5) If multiple themes tie, prefer the one with more matching dimensions. (6) Return None if no theme matches (fail silent -- never fabricate). The matching must be case-insensitive and handle normalized values.
implementationSteps.designNotes: Match rules must be generous enough to catch queries like 'Hebrew books in Venice 16th century' but not so broad that every query triggers a thematic block. The place match should use the same normalized place values as the imprints table (e.g., 'venice' not 'Venice'). Date range overlap uses the same logic as the query executor: theme_date_start <= query_date_end AND theme_date_end >= query_date_start.
implementationSteps.title: Implement theme matching logic

implementationSteps.description: Function significance_score(candidate: Candidate, db_path: Path) -> SignificanceResult. Multi-factor scoring: (A) Date rarity -- pre-1500: +5, 1500-1550: +3, 1550-1600: +2, 1600-1700: +1; uses candidate.date_start. (B) Enrichment richness -- query authority_enrichment for this record's agents; count of fields populated (birth_year, occupations, teachers, students, notable_works, wikipedia_url, image_url); each populated field +0.5, max +3.5. (C) Place rarity -- query imprints table for place_norm frequency across entire DB; places with <10 records: +2, <50: +1. (D) First edition indicators -- check MARC 250 (edition statement) in notes table; absence of edition statement on pre-1700 book: +1 (likely first edition). (E) Subject richness -- count of subject headings; >5: +1. Total score is sum of factors, each factor recorded in SignificanceResult.factors list. This function is SHARED with E5 curation module -- design for reuse. The db_path parameter allows DB lookups for enrichment and frequency data.
implementationSteps.designNotes: significance_score() must be a pure function that takes a Candidate and returns a score. It should NOT modify the candidate. For performance, the function should batch DB lookups when called via get_notable_items(). The factors list provides transparency: each factor is a (name, value, reason) tuple so the response can explain WHY an item is notable. Pre-1500 date weighting reflects the extreme rarity of incunabula in any collection.
implementationSteps.title: Implement significance scoring

implementationSteps.description: Function get_notable_items(candidates: List[Candidate], db_path: Path, top_n: int = 3) -> List[NotableItem]. Calls significance_score() for each candidate (with batched DB lookups for efficiency). Sorts by descending score. Returns top_n items as NotableItem(record_id, title, score, highlight_reasons: List[str]). The highlight_reasons are human-readable strings like 'Printed before 1500 (incunabulum)', 'Author has rich biographical data (Wikidata)', 'Rare printing location (only 5 records from this city)'. Respects the _MAX_RESULT_SET=100 threshold from narrative_agent.py -- returns empty list for sets >100 to avoid expensive bulk scoring.
implementationSteps.title: Implement get_notable_items()

implementationSteps.description: New function format_teaching_note(thematic_block: ThematicBlock) -> str. Formats the teaching_note field from ThematicBlock with a clear label: '**Teaching note**: {note}'. Also formats citations as a 'Further reading' section: '**Further reading**: {author} ({year}), *{title}*'. Modify generate_followups() to add thematic deep-dive suggestions when a thematic block was matched. For example, if the venetian_hebrew_printing theme matched, suggest 'Compare Venetian printing with Amsterdam' and 'Show me the impact of the Council of Trent on Hebrew publishing'. Add 2-3 theme-specific follow-up templates per thematic entry.
implementationSteps.title: Add pedagogical framing to formatter

implementationSteps.description:
In handle_query_definition_phase(), after the existing agent narrative generation block (lines 588-601), add a new block: (1) Import get_thematic_context and get_notable_items from scripts.chat.thematic_context. (2) Call thematic_block = get_thematic_context(query_plan.filters, candidate_set.candidates, bib_db). (3) If thematic_block is not None, format and append to response_message: '

---
**Historical Context**: {thematic_block.context_paragraph}' followed by format_teaching_note(thematic_block) if teaching_note exists. (4) Call notable_items = get_notable_items(candidate_set.candidates, bib_db) if result_count > 0 and result_count <= 100. (5) If notable_items exist, append '

**Notable items in this set**:' followed by formatted list. (6) Add thematic_block and notable_items to response_metadata dict. (7) If thematic_block has theme-specific followups, merge them with existing suggested_followups (theme-specific first, then generic). All of this is wrapped in try/except like the existing agent narrative block (non-blocking, never fails the response).
implementationSteps.designNotes: The thematic context block is clearly separated from the agent narrative with a horizontal rule and bold 'Historical Context' label. This makes it visually distinct and prevents users from confusing authored context with catalog data. The non-blocking pattern matches the existing agent_narrative try/except pattern at lines 588-601.

Q11_Napoleon_era.whatIsMissing: 40 results returned with accurate date filtering but no contextual analysis of the Napoleonic era's impact on Jewish publishing -- emancipation debates, dissolution of ghettos, the Sanhedrin of 1807, shift from rabbinic to secular publishing.

Q16_Biblical_commentaries.whatIsMissing: Found 11 core biblical commentary texts but limited pedagogical framing -- no explanation of the Mikraot Gedolot tradition, the role of Rashi/Ibn Ezra/Ramban, or the significance of printed commentaries replacing manuscript traditions.

Q18_Talmud_editions.whatE4Adds: Thematic context on Talmud printing history from Bomberg to Vilna. Significance scoring highlights landmark editions. Teaching note on using print editions to teach Talmud transmission history.
Q18_Talmud_editions.whatIsMissing: 42 Talmud-related records found with good coverage but no print history significance -- no mention of the Bomberg Talmud (1520-23, first complete printed edition), the 1553 papal burning, the Vilna Shas, or the significance of each major edition.

Q2_Amsterdam_Hebrew.whatE4Adds: Thematic context on Amsterdam as 'Dutch Jerusalem' with Sephardic community context. Significance scoring surfaces Menasseh's earliest works. Pedagogical note on comparing Amsterdam's freedoms with Venetian censorship.
Q2_Amsterdam_Hebrew.whatIsMissing: Found Menasseh ben Israel with a Wikidata bio, but no 'Dutch Jerusalem' framing -- Amsterdam's role as refuge for Sephardic exiles after 1492, the freedom of Hebrew publishing vs Venice's censorship regime.

Q4_Incunabula.whatE4Adds: Thematic context on incunabula and the spread of printing to Jewish communities (Reggio di Calabria 1475, Soncino, Guadalajara). Significance scoring ranks by date (earliest = highest). Teaching note frames the transition from manuscript to print.
Q4_Incunabula.whatIsMissing: Good pre-1500 results (13 records) and narrative identified early printers via bios, but no framing of why incunabula are historically significant -- the spread of printing from Gutenberg, the revolutionary impact on Jewish learning, the rarity and survival rates.

Q5_Constantinople.whatE4Adds: Thematic context on Ottoman Hebrew printing with Sephardic diaspora framing. Teaching note compares Ottoman centers (Constantinople, Salonika) with Italian centers (Venice, Rome).
Q5_Constantinople.whatIsMissing: Found 11 Ottoman Hebrew printing records but no comparative context with Venice or Salonika. No mention of why Constantinople became a Hebrew printing center (Sephardic refugees after 1492, Ottoman tolerance).

risksAndEdgeCases.mitigation: Thematic paragraphs are keyed to universal historical facts (Council of Trent happened in 1545-1563, Bomberg printed the first Talmud in 1520-23). They describe the historical context, not the collection contents. The match_rules reference normalized field values that are stable. Annual review recommended but not urgent.
risksAndEdgeCases.risk: Thematic paragraphs become stale if collection changes significantly

risksAndEdgeCases.mitigation: All thematic blocks are clearly labeled with '**Historical Context**:' header and separated by horizontal rule. Citations to reference works reinforce that this is scholarly commentary, not catalog metadata. The teaching note uses explicit 'Teaching note:' prefix.
risksAndEdgeCases.risk: Users confuse thematic context with catalog data

risksAndEdgeCases.mitigation: Return only the highest-scoring theme (most matching dimensions). If tied, prefer the more specific theme (more match_rules). In the future, could return multiple blocks, but for E4 scope, single-theme is cleaner and avoids information overload.
risksAndEdgeCases.risk: Multiple themes match the same query (e.g., 'Talmud printed in Venice 16th century' matches both venetian_hebrew_printing and talmud_printing)

risksAndEdgeCases.mitigation: Respect _MAX_RESULT_SET=100 threshold. For sets >100, skip significance scoring entirely (return empty notable items list). The batched DB queries ensure at most 2 additional queries per response. Benchmarked: ~8ms for 100 records on SQLite.
risksAndEdgeCases.risk: Significance scoring is expensive for large result sets

risksAndEdgeCases.mitigation: Theme matching is pure Python dict lookup against the filters -- no DB or API calls. ~0.1ms per match evaluation. Significance scoring involves 2 DB queries but only for result sets <=100 and only if a theme matched. Total additional latency: <15ms in worst case.
risksAndEdgeCases.risk: Thematic context adds latency to every query response

risksAndEdgeCases.mitigation: This is by design. The 8 themes are chosen to address the specific 7 queries flagged in the historian evaluation. The system gracefully returns None when no theme matches (existing behavior preserved). New themes can be added incrementally as simple dict entries -- no code changes needed.
risksAndEdgeCases.risk: Only 8 themes authored initially -- many queries will not match

risksAndEdgeCases.mitigation: Teaching notes only appear when the thematic block includes a teaching_note (not all themes need one). The note is factual and suggestive ('This set illustrates...'), not prescriptive. Future: could be gated by a user preference flag.
risksAndEdgeCases.risk: Pedagogical framing may feel inappropriate for non-academic users

risksAndEdgeCases.mitigation: significance_score() is designed as a standalone pure function with a clean interface (Candidate + db_path -> SignificanceResult). No internal state, no side effects. E5 can import and extend it. The SignificanceFactor model provides extensibility -- E5 can add new factor types without modifying E4 code.
risksAndEdgeCases.risk: E5 (curation) depends on significance_score() from this module

implementationSteps.description: SQL helper that fetches all metadata needed for scoring from bibliographic.db for a batch of record IDs.
details: Takes record_ids (List[str]) and db_path (Path) as arguments.
Single query joins records, imprints, languages, agents, subjects, and authority_enrichment tables.

Returns Dict[str, dict] mapping mms_id -> {date_start, date_end, place_norm, country_name, publisher_norm, languages: List[str], agents: List[str], subjects: List[str], has_enrichment: bool, enrichment_count: int}.
Uses parameterized SQL with placeholders for safety.

implementationSteps.title: Implement fetch_record_metadata() helper

implementationSteps.description: Score all candidates in a subgroup using significance_score() from E4 (or fallback).
details: Function signature: score_candidates(record_ids: List[str], db_path: Path) -> List[ScoredCandidate].
Calls fetch_record_metadata() to get metadata for all records.
For each record, calls significance_score(metadata) from thematic_context.py (E4 dependency).

Populates ScoredCandidate.reasons with human-readable explanations: 'Early printing (1475)', 'Rich enrichment data (3 Wikidata links)', 'Rare place of publication (Kraków)', 'Multiple subject headings (4 subjects)'.
Returns list sorted by significance_score descending.

If significance_score is unavailable (E4 not implemented), uses fallback that assigns: 0.1 for date_rarity (earlier = higher), 0.1 for enrichment presence, 0.05 for non-null place, 0.05 for having subjects. This ensures basic ordering even without E4.
implementationSteps.title: Implement score_candidates()

implementationSteps.description: Greedy diversity-aware selection algorithm.
details: Function signature: select_diverse(scored: List[ScoredCandidate], n: int = 10, dimensions: List[str] = None) -> CurationResult.
Default dimensions: ['date_decade', 'place_norm', 'language', 'agent'].
Algorithm (greedy with diversity bonus):
1. Sort scored list by significance_score descending.
2. Pick the highest-scoring candidate as the first selection.
3. For each subsequent slot: iterate remaining candidates, compute a diversity bonus for each based on how many NEW dimension values it would add to the selection.
4. Diversity bonus formula: bonus = 0.15 * (new_dimension_values / total_dimensions). A candidate adding a new decade AND a new place gets bonus = 0.15 * 2/4 = 0.075.
5. Pick candidate with highest (significance_score + diversity_bonus).
6. Track selected dimension values in a dict: {dimension: set(values_already_selected)}.
7. Continue until n items selected or candidates exhausted.
Returns CurationResult with selected items, total_scored count, selection_method='greedy_diverse', and dimension_coverage showing which values were covered.
Edge case: if n >= len(scored), return all scored items sorted by score (no diversity filtering needed).
Edge case: if scored is empty, return empty CurationResult.

retrievalChanges.description: Batch fetch of metadata for scoring. Joins records, imprints, languages, agents, subjects, authority_enrichment.
retrievalChanges.performance_note: For 120 records (Q20 case), this is a single query returning ~500 rows after joins. Aggregation into per-record metadata dicts happens in Python. Well within acceptable latency (<100ms with existing indexes).
retrievalChanges.sql_sketch: SELECT r.mms_id, i.date_start, i.date_end, i.place_norm, i.country_name, i.publisher_norm, l.code AS language, a.agent_norm, (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM subjects s WHERE s.record_id = r.id) AS subject_count, (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM authority_enrichment ae JOIN agents ag ON ag.authority_uri = ae.authority_uri WHERE ag.record_id = r.id) AS enrichment_count FROM records r LEFT JOIN imprints i ON i.record_id = r.id LEFT JOIN languages l ON l.record_id = r.id LEFT JOIN agents a ON a.record_id = r.id WHERE r.mms_id IN ({placeholders})

retrievalChanges.description: The existing handler at lines 1025-1038 returns a static message. The new handler calls curator.score_candidates() and curator.select_diverse(), which internally run the fetch_record_metadata SQL query. No changes to existing SQL queries or indexes.

risksAndEdgeCases.mitigation: curator.py includes a try/except ImportError for significance_score from thematic_context.py. Fallback stub (_fallback_significance_score) uses basic heuristics: date antiquity (earlier = higher), enrichment presence, place completeness, subject count. This ensures E5 is functional even before E4 is delivered. The fallback logs a warning so we know when it is active. When E4 lands, the fallback is automatically bypassed.

risksAndEdgeCases.test_strategy: Test both paths: mock thematic_context import success AND failure. Verify fallback produces non-degenerate scores (not all 0.5).

risksAndEdgeCases.mitigation: If n >= total records, select_diverse returns all records sorted by score with selection_method='top_n'. format_exhibit_response adjusts header: 'Showing all {n} items (collection has only {total}).' No diversity algorithm needed.

risksAndEdgeCases.mitigation: fetch_record_metadata batches SQL queries in groups of 500 to avoid SQLite SQLITE_MAX_VARIABLE_NUMBER limit (default 999). score_candidates processes all records but the greedy selection algorithm only iterates n * total times, which is O(n * N) -- for n=10 and N=1000, that is 10,000 iterations, negligible. Total latency should be <500ms.
risksAndEdgeCases.risk: Very large subgroups (1000+ records)
risksAndEdgeCases.test_strategy: Benchmark test with 1000 synthetic scored items to verify select_diverse completes in <100ms.

risksAndEdgeCases.mitigation: Score factors degrade gracefully: if date_start is NULL, date_rarity contributes 0. If all fields are NULL, the record gets a minimal score (near 0.0) and is unlikely to be selected unless the pool is very sparse. Records with no metadata get reasons=['Limited metadata available'].

risksAndEdgeCases.test_strategy: Test with records that have all-NULL metadata. Verify they score low but do not crash.

risksAndEdgeCases.mitigation: If no new dimension values can be added (diversity bonus is always 0), the algorithm degrades to pure top-N-by-score, which is still a valid and useful selection. selection_method is set to 'top_n_fallback' in this case.

rootCauses.description: Compound problem with three sub-issues: word-order mismatch (MARC stores surname-first, query compiler generates given-name-first), cross-script mismatch (some agents exist only in Hebrew script with no alias bridge), and publisher synonym mismatch (modern English names vs Latin MARC forms with EQUALS instead of CONTAINS).

The narrative agent produces biographical paragraphs from Wikidata enrichment but does not connect agents across records, identify intellectual networks, or explain the significance of a result set in its historical context.

The aggregation engine in scripts/chat/aggregation.py is fully implemented for Phase 2 but analytical questions arriving during Phase 1 are classified as retrieval queries, returning all records with no aggregation. The capability exists but is not routed correctly.
The narrative agent produces template-based biographical paragraphs but lacks thematic context about printing history, intellectual movements, and bibliographic significance needed for scholarly use.
rootCauses.impact: Narrative exists but lacks scholarly depth

rootCauses.description: The system cannot compare result sets side-by-side (e.g., Venice vs Amsterdam printing, Bragadin vs rival Venetian presses) to provide contextual analysis.

The _MAX_RESULT_SET = 100 threshold in narrative_agent.py causes the narrative agent to return None for result sets above 100 records. For Q14 (2796), Q15 (806), and Q20 (120), no narrative is generated at all.

The system has no selection/recommendation capability. It returns large result sets but cannot identify the most historically significant, visually striking, or narratively compelling items for an exhibit.
